criteria.criterion: <fact>Contributions  Centralized benchmark: We train various binary classification algorithms, including logistic regression (LR), neural networks (NN), support vector machines (SVM), k-nearest neighbor (KNN), Na¨ıve Bayes (NB), and random forests.</fact> <scope>from paper context</scope>
criteria.fact: Contributions  Centralized benchmark: We train various binary classification algorithms, including logistic regression (LR), neural networks (NN), support vector machines (SVM), k-nearest neighbor (KNN), Na¨ıve Bayes (NB), and random forests.

criteria.criterion: <fact>In particular, we use the top three performing models in the centralized setup as local trainers for the federated setting.</fact> <scope>from paper context</scope>
criteria.fact: In particular, we use the top three performing models in the centralized setup as local trainers for the federated setting.

criteria.criterion: <fact>We examine various federated aggregation  strategies including FedAvg [9], FedAdam [16], FedYogi [16], and SCAFFOLD [14].</fact> <scope>from paper context</scope>
criteria.fact: We examine various federated aggregation  strategies including FedAvg [9], FedAdam [16], FedYogi [16], and SCAFFOLD [14].

criteria.criterion: <fact>To meet our specific needs, we develop our own version of federated SVM from scratch.</fact> <scope>from paper context</scope>
criteria.fact: To meet our specific needs, we develop our own version of federated SVM from scratch.

criteria.criterion: <fact>For a fair comparison, we follow the data preprocessing steps from [7], including removing entries with missing values (reducing the dataset to 740 points), using a $6 6 \% - 3 4 \%$ train-test split, converting labels $\mathbf { \dot { \theta } } 1 - 4 \mathbf { \overrightarrow { \theta } }$ to ”1” for binary classification (0 indicating no heart disease and 1 for heart disease), and normalizing the dataset.</fact> <scope>from paper context</scope>

criteria.criterion: <fact>By dynamically adjusting  # Algorithm 1 Local classifier  Require: Dataset $D$ (920 points)  1: Set hyperparameter values for chosen model    2: Remove points with missing values from $D$    3: Split $D$ into $66 \%$ training set $T$ and $34 \%$ test set $E$    4: Normalize $T$ (e.g., scale features to have zero mean and unit variance)    5: Initialize model parameters $\theta$    6: for $t = 1$ to 50 epochs do    7: Update model parameters $\theta$ by training on $T$    8: end for    9: Evaluate final model on test set $E$    10: Compute testing accuracy Acc    11: Output Acc as the final testing accuracy  # Algorithm 2 Federated aggregation  Require: Dataset D from 4 clients $( D _ { 1 } , D _ { 2 } , D _ { 3 } , D _ { 4 } )$  1: Set hyperparameter values for chosen model    2: Initialize global model parameters $\theta$    3: for each client $k \in \{ 1 , 2 , 3 , 4 \}$ do    4: Preprocess data similar to Local Classifier    5: for $t = 1$ to 50 epochs do    6: Local Classifier    7: Send $\theta _ { k }$ to the server    8: Receive updated global model parameters $\theta$ from the server    9: end for    10: Evaluate local model on test set $E _ { k }$    11: Compute local testing accuracy $A c c _ { k }$    12: end for    13: Aggregate local testing accuracies $A c c _ { 1 } , A c c _ { 2 } , A c c _ { 3 } , A c c _ { 4 }$    14: PCompute average testing accuracy4k=1 nkn Acck $\begin{array} { r l } { \operatorname { A c c } _ { \mathrm { a v g } } \quad } & { { } = } \end{array}$ -nAcck    15: Output $\mathbf { A c c _ { a v g } }$ as the final testing accuracy  learning rates based on the magnitude and direction of gradients, ADAM optimizes the training process for each client individually, contributing to more accurate model updates.</fact> <scope>from paper context</scope>
criteria.fact: By dynamically adjusting  # Algorithm 1 Local classifier  Require: Dataset $D$ (920 points)  1: Set hyperparameter values for chosen model    2: Remove points with missing values from $D$    3: Split $D$ into $66 \%$ training set $T$ and $34 \%$ test set $E$    4: Normalize $T$ (e.g., scale features to have zero mean and unit variance)    5: Initialize model parameters $\theta$    6: for $t = 1$ to 50 epochs do    7: Update model parameters $\theta$ by training on $T$    8: end for    9: Evaluate final model on test set $E$    10: Compute testing accuracy Acc    11: Output Acc as the final testing accuracy  # Algorithm 2 Federated aggregation  Require: Dataset D from 4 clients $( D _ { 1 } , D _ { 2 } , D _ { 3 } , D _ { 4 } )$  1: Set hyperparameter values for chosen model    2: Initialize global model parameters $\theta$    3: for each client $k \in \{ 1 , 2 , 3 , 4 \}$ do    4: Preprocess data similar to Local Classifier    5: for $t = 1$ to 50 epochs do    6: Local Classifier    7: Send $\theta _ { k }$ to the server    8: Receive updated global model parameters $\theta$ from the server    9: end for    10: Evaluate local model on test set $E _ { k }$    11: Compute local testing accuracy $A c c _ { k }$    12: end for    13: Aggregate local testing accuracies $A c c _ { 1 } , A c c _ { 2 } , A c c _ { 3 } , A c c _ { 4 }$    14: PCompute average testing accuracy4k=1 nkn Acck $\begin{array} { r l } { \operatorname { A c c } _ { \mathrm { a v g } } \quad } & { { } = } \end{array}$ -nAcck    15: Output $\mathbf { A c c _ { a v g } }$ as the final testing accuracy  learning rates based on the magnitude and direction of gradients, ADAM optimizes the training process for each client individually, contributing to more accurate model updates.

criteria.criterion: <fact>Client updates are computed as:  $$ \begin{array}{l} \Delta_ {t} = \frac {1}{| S |} \sum_ {i \in S} \Delta_ {i} ^ {t} \\ m _ {t} = \beta_ {1} m _ {t - 1} + (1 - \beta_ {1}) \Delta_ {t} \tag {1} \\ v _ {t} = \beta_ {2} v _ {t - 1} + (1 - \beta_ {2}) (\Delta_ {t}) ^ {2} \\ x _ {t + 1} = x _ {t} - \eta \frac {m _ {t}}{\sqrt {v _ {t}} + \tau} \\ \end{array} $$  where $\Delta _ { t }$ is the aggregated gradient update at time t, $S$ is the set of clients participating, $| S |$ is the number of clients in $S$ , $m _ { t }$ and $v _ { t }$ are the first and second moment estimates, $\beta _ { 1 }$ and $\beta _ { 2 }$ are the exponential decay rates for these moment estimates, $\eta$ is the learning rate, and $\tau$ is a small constant for numerical stability.</fact> <scope>from paper context</scope>
criteria.fact: Client updates are computed as:  $$ \begin{array}{l} \Delta_ {t} = \frac {1}{| S |} \sum_ {i \in S} \Delta_ {i} ^ {t} \\ m _ {t} = \beta_ {1} m _ {t - 1} + (1 - \beta_ {1}) \Delta_ {t} \tag {1} \\ v _ {t} = \beta_ {2} v _ {t - 1} + (1 - \beta_ {2}) (\Delta_ {t}) ^ {2} \\ x _ {t + 1} = x _ {t} - \eta \frac {m _ {t}}{\sqrt {v _ {t}} + \tau} \\ \end{array} $$  where $\Delta _ { t }$ is the aggregated gradient update at time t, $S$ is the set of clients participating, $| S |$ is the number of clients in $S$ , $m _ { t }$ and $v _ { t }$ are the first and second moment estimates, $\beta _ { 1 }$ and $\beta _ { 2 }$ are the exponential decay rates for these moment estimates, $\eta$ is the learning rate, and $\tau$ is a small constant for numerical stability.

criteria.criterion: <fact>Specifically, to address the issue of heterogeneity, FedYogi dynamically adjusts the learning rate based on the gradients’ first and second moments, $m _ { t }$ and $v _ { t }$ , respectively.</fact> <scope>from paper context</scope>
criteria.fact: Specifically, to address the issue of heterogeneity, FedYogi dynamically adjusts the learning rate based on the gradients’ first and second moments, $m _ { t }$ and $v _ { t }$ , respectively.

criteria.criterion: <fact>For each client, the updates are computed similarly to FedAdam, except for the second moment estimate, $v _ { t }$ , which is computed as  $$ v _ {t} = v _ {t - 1} - (1 - \beta_ {2}) \Delta_ {t} ^ {2} \mathrm {s i g n} (v _ {t - 1} - \Delta_ {t} ^ {2}) $$  where $v _ { t } , \beta _ { 2 } , \Delta _ { t }$ are the same as in (1).</fact> <scope>from paper context</scope>
criteria.fact: For each client, the updates are computed similarly to FedAdam, except for the second moment estimate, $v _ { t }$ , which is computed as  $$ v _ {t} = v _ {t - 1} - (1 - \beta_ {2}) \Delta_ {t} ^ {2} \mathrm {s i g n} (v _ {t - 1} - \Delta_ {t} ^ {2}) $$  where $v _ { t } , \beta _ { 2 } , \Delta _ { t }$ are the same as in (1).

criteria.criterion: <fact>The difference in how $v _ { t }$ is computed between the two strategies is that when $v _ { t - 1 }$ is much larger than $\Delta _ { t } ^ { 2 }$ , both strategies will rapidly increase the learning rate, but FedYogi does so in a more controlled way [48].</fact> <scope>from paper context</scope>
criteria.fact: The difference in how $v _ { t }$ is computed between the two strategies is that when $v _ { t - 1 }$ is much larger than $\Delta _ { t } ^ { 2 }$ , both strategies will rapidly increase the learning rate, but FedYogi does so in a more controlled way [48].

criteria.criterion: <fact>Updates for the global and local control variables and model parameters are computed as follows:  $$ \begin{array}{l} \boldsymbol {y} _ {i} \leftarrow \boldsymbol {y} _ {i} - \eta_ {l} \left(g _ {i} \left(\boldsymbol {y} _ {i}\right) + \boldsymbol {c} - \boldsymbol {c} _ {i}\right) \\ c _ {i} ^ {+} \leftarrow (\mathrm {i}) g _ {i} (\boldsymbol {x}), \text {o r (i i)} \boldsymbol {c} _ {i} - \boldsymbol {c} + \frac {1}{K \eta_ {l}} (\boldsymbol {x} - \boldsymbol {y} _ {i}) \\ \boldsymbol {x} \leftarrow \boldsymbol {x} + \frac {\eta_ {g}}{| S |} \sum_ {i \in S} (\boldsymbol {y} _ {i} - \boldsymbol {x}) \\ \boldsymbol {c} \gets \boldsymbol {c} + \frac {1}{N} \sum_ {i \in S} (\boldsymbol {c} _ {\boldsymbol {i}} ^ {+} - \boldsymbol {c} _ {\boldsymbol {i}}) \\ \end{array} $$  where $\mathbf { { \mathit { y } } } _ { i }$ is client update performed $K$ times, $\eta$ is the learning rate, $g _ { i } ( y _ { i } )$ is the mini-batch gradient, and $_ { \textbf { \em x } }$ are the server parameters.</fact> <scope>from paper context</scope>
criteria.fact: Updates for the global and local control variables and model parameters are computed as follows:  $$ \begin{array}{l} \boldsymbol {y} _ {i} \leftarrow \boldsymbol {y} _ {i} - \eta_ {l} \left(g _ {i} \left(\boldsymbol {y} _ {i}\right) + \boldsymbol {c} - \boldsymbol {c} _ {i}\right) \\ c _ {i} ^ {+} \leftarrow (\mathrm {i}) g _ {i} (\boldsymbol {x}), \text {o r (i i)} \boldsymbol {c} _ {i} - \boldsymbol {c} + \frac {1}{K \eta_ {l}} (\boldsymbol {x} - \boldsymbol {y} _ {i}) \\ \boldsymbol {x} \leftarrow \boldsymbol {x} + \frac {\eta_ {g}}{| S |} \sum_ {i \in S} (\boldsymbol {y} _ {i} - \boldsymbol {x}) \\ \boldsymbol {c} \gets \boldsymbol {c} + \frac {1}{N} \sum_ {i \in S} (\boldsymbol {c} _ {\boldsymbol {i}} ^ {+} - \boldsymbol {c} _ {\boldsymbol {i}}) \\ \end{array} $$  where $\mathbf { { \mathit { y } } } _ { i }$ is client update performed $K$ times, $\eta$ is the learning rate, $g _ { i } ( y _ { i } )$ is the mini-batch gradient, and $_ { \textbf { \em x } }$ are the server parameters.

criteria.criterion: <fact>Notice there are two options to compute the local control variate $c _ { i }$ .</fact> <scope>from paper context</scope>
criteria.fact: Notice there are two options to compute the local control variate $c _ { i }$ .

criteria.criterion: <fact>Option (i) makes an additional pass over the client’s local data to compute the gradient at server model $_ { \textbf { \em x } }$ .</fact> <scope>from paper context</scope>
criteria.fact: Option (i) makes an additional pass over the client’s local data to compute the gradient at server model $_ { \textbf { \em x } }$ .

criteria.criterion: <fact>Option (ii) reuses the previously computed gradients, making it the cheaper but less stable option.</fact> <scope>from paper context</scope>
criteria.fact: Option (ii) reuses the previously computed gradients, making it the cheaper but less stable option.

# IV. from paper context

<fact>INTERPRETABILITY ANALYSIS  In critical applications like healthcare, interpretability is essential for understanding the decision-making of predictive models.</fact> <scope>from paper context</scope>
criteria.fact: INTERPRETABILITY ANALYSIS  In critical applications like healthcare, interpretability is essential for understanding the decision-making of predictive models.

criteria.criterion: <fact>Model complexity effect on Shapley-based interpretability.: The unexpected SHAP values for Decision Trees (DT) and K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) are due to their inherent complexity compared to simpler linear models.</fact> <scope>from paper context</scope>
criteria.fact: Model complexity effect on Shapley-based interpretability.: The unexpected SHAP values for Decision Trees (DT) and K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) are due to their inherent complexity compared to simpler linear models.

criteria.criterion: <fact>DTs create partitions based on features, complicating the assignment of SHAP values as each split affects the prediction path.</fact> <scope>from paper context</scope>
criteria.fact: DTs create partitions based on features, complicating the assignment of SHAP values as each split affects the prediction path.

criteria.fact: In contrast, KNN considers instances relative to their nearest neighbors, leading to variability in SHAP values because predictions depend on different neighbor sets.

criteria.fact: This variability makes interpreting SHAP values difficult, as feature contributions are not consistently defined across instances.

criteria.criterion: <fact>CONCLUSION  In this work, we established a benchmark for binary classification models for heart disease using the UCI heart disease dataset, evaluated in both centralized (pooled) and federated settings, and conducted interpretability analysis using Shapley values to understand the features influencing model predictions.</fact> <scope>from paper context</scope>
criteria.fact: CONCLUSION  In this work, we established a benchmark for binary classification models for heart disease using the UCI heart disease dataset, evaluated in both centralized (pooled) and federated settings, and conducted interpretability analysis using Shapley values to understand the features influencing model predictions.
